macro_segments.title: Introduction to Bechor, Ma'aser, and Pesach as Kodashim Kalim

micro_segments.title: Overview of three types of Kodashim Kalim: Bechor for Kohanim, Ma'aser for owners, Pesach with specific eating parameters

macro_segments.title: Source for Bechor eating period: Two days and one intervening night

micro_segments.title: Tannaitic source: linking Bechor to Chazeh and Shok of Shelamim to derive two-day eating period
Question at Yavneh: how long is Bechor eaten? Rabbi Tarfon's initial answer
Rabbi Yose HaGalili challenges Tarfon: proposing comparison to Chatat and Asham instead
Rabbi Tarfon's response: Bechor and Shelamim both not brought for sin, both Kodashim Kalim
Rabbi Yose HaGalili counters: Bechor, Chatat, and Asham all gifts to Kohen, not brought as Nedavah

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva and Rabbi Yishmael resolve the debate through exegetical analysis of Uvesaram Yihye Lach

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Akiva's intervention: explicit text links Bechor to Chazeh and Shok of Shelamim, not Toda
Rabbi Yose HaGalili responds: perhaps Chazeh and Shok of Toda, not Shelamim
Rabbi Akiva's resolution: repetition of 'Lach Yihye' extends to full Shelamim period, not Toda
Rabbi Yishmael's correction: Akiva conceded incorrectly; Chazeh and Shok exist only through Shelamim, so cannot apply to Toda
Principle: Davar halamad b'Heikesh Ein Chozer u'Melamed b'Heikesh - cannot double-apply derived knowledge

macro_segments.title: Application of Lach Yihye to Bechor with Mum

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yishmael's use of Lach Yihye: teaches that Bechor with Mum still given to Kohen without Korban
Rabbi Akiva's alternative: derives from plural Besaram that both Tam and Ba'al Mum are included
Rabbi Yishmael's interpretation: Besaram refers to all Bechorot types, Mum derived from Lach Yihye

macro_segments.title: Debate on Himenu v'Davar Acher and double Heikesh application

micro_segments.title: Akiva's reasoning: part already in Bechor, so applying learning from Shelamim to Toda is permitted
Yishmael's view: principle applies even when part is already present in source category
Comparison case: Bechor inside Kodesh Kodashim to outside via Pesach Kapparah blood sprinklings
Resolution: when primary halacha already exists in target, can apply secondary heikesh-derived element

macro_segments.title: Case of Shtei HaLechem to Toda bread requirements

micro_segments.title: Linking Shtei HaLechem to Toda Chametz loaves via Heikesh for proper portion sizes
Extending from Chametz to Matza loaves via additional link, testing double Heikesh limits
Resolution: Taviu is redundant, teaching direct rule without Heikesh, allowing downstream application

macro_segments.title: Pesach eaten only until Chatzot: source and dispute

micro_segments.title: Attribution to Rabbi Elazar ben Azarya: links Pesach night to Maka Bechorot timing
Rabbi Akiva's counterpoint: text says eaten in haste, until morning, whole night permitted
Akiva explains 'Balayla Hazeh' distinguishes from day-eating of other Kodashim, not limiting time
Abaye's question: why not interpret as Derabbanan like other Kodashim Kalim?
Answer: phrasing 'Ein Ne'echal Ella' indicates absolute limit (De'oraita), unlike other cases

topical_tags.term: Chatat and Asham (sin and guilt offerings)
Chazeh and Shok (breast and thigh portions)
Two days and one intervening night (eating period)
Davar halamad b'Heikesh Ein Chozer u'Melamed b'Heikesh (cannot double-apply derived principle)
Himenu v'Davar Acher (part already exists, part newly learned)
